text: Draft, review, publish Civilytics / jaredknowles.com blog. Cross-post: R-bloggers (via blog’s R feed/tag), Mastodon/Bluesky, rOpenSci/Rweekly appropriate. Starter outline (talking points): Hook — merTools CRAN since 2015 (~457K downloads, ~8K/month). 1.0 marks feature complete start long-term-support phase. — fast, simulation-based prediction intervals lme4 mixed models (predictInterval()), bootMer() / full MCMC impractical; plus tools extract, simulate, visualize effects. Correctness fix: reproducible predictInterval() nested/interaction random effects (#124). New new.levels = "draw" — sample unobserved groups’ effects RE covariance (matches brms allow_new_levels). New plotREimpact(); plotFEsim() highlights significant terms. shinyMer() revived extended (model-summary tab, subset draws). Refreshed docs: new Contextual Effects vignette proper citation. headline — validated brms. predictInterval reproduces full-Bayesian posterior prediction intervals almost exactly (point estimates cor 0.9998, matching 90% intervals coverage) ~1/400th cost. Link/screenshot new “Validating predictInterval() brms” vignette calibration figure. LTS / maintenance mode — stable API, decade use; future work bug fixes, CRAN/dependency compatibility, docs, churn. Call action — install.packages("merTools"), read site (https://jknowles.github.io/merTools/), cite (citation("merTools")), file issues. Thank contributors (Carl Frederick, Alex Whitworth, bbolker, DavisVaughan, reporters like dotPiano #124).

Finally, indicate want predictions incorporate residual variance model – option available lmerMod objects. first rows object PI: three columns median (fit) limits 95% prediction interval (upr lwr) set level=0.95. following figure displays output graphically first 30 observations. plot chunk Inspect_predInt_2
code: set.seed(271828) data(sleepstudy) fm1 <- lmer(Reaction ~ Days + (Days|Subject), data=sleepstudy) arm::display(fm1) #> lmer(formula = Reaction ~ Days + (Days | Subject), data = sleepstudy) #>             coef.est coef.se #> (Intercept) 251.41     6.82  #> Days         10.47     1.55  #>  #> Error terms: #>  Groups   Name        Std.Dev. Corr  #>  Subject  (Intercept) 24.74          #>           Days         5.92    0.07  #>  Residual             25.59          #> --- #> number of obs: 180, groups: Subject, 18 #> AIC = 1755.6, DIC = 1760.3 #> deviance = 1751.9 PI.time <- system.time(   PI <- predictInterval(merMod = fm1, newdata = sleepstudy,                         level = 0.95, n.sims = 1000,                         stat = "median", type="linear.prediction",                         include.resid.var = TRUE) ) library(ggplot2); ggplot(aes(x=1:30, y=fit, ymin=lwr, ymax=upr), data=PI[1:30,]) +   geom_point() +   geom_linerange() +   labs(x="Index", y="Prediction w/ 95% PI") + theme_bw()

title: Prediction Intervals from merMod Objects
text: prediction intervals correct correlations fixed random effects. tends lead predictive intervals conservative, especially existing groups lot data relatively groups. case, significant portion uncertainty prediction can due variance fixed intercept anti-correlated variance random intercept effects. instance, actually matter fixed intercept 5 random intercept effects -2, 1, 1, versus fixed intercept 6 random intercept effects -3, 0, 0. (latter situation never MLE, can occur package’s simulations.) show issue, ’ll use sleep study model, predicting reaction times subjects experiencing sleep deprivation: Let’s use model give interval true average body fat large group students like first one study — 196cm female baseball player: two ways get predictInterval create less-conservative intervals deal . first just tell consider certain fixed effects fully-known (, effectively 0 variance.) done using ignore.fixed.effects argument. second way use ad-hoc variance adjustment, fix.intercept.variance argument. takes model’s intercept variance σ̂μ2\hat\sigma^2_\mu adjusts : σ̂′μ2=σ̂μ2−Σlevels1Σgroups(level)1/(σ̂level2+sigmagroup2)\hat\sigma\prime^2_\mu = \hat\sigma^2_\mu-\Sigma_{levels}\frac{1}{\Sigma_{groups(level)}1/(\hat\sigma^2_{level}+sigma^2_{group})} words, assumes given intercept variance incorporates spurious variance level, spurious variance terms precision equal precisions due individual groups level. notes two arguments: fix.intercept.variance=T redundant ignore.fixed.effects=1, vice versa. corrections used predicting outcomes groups present original data.
code: fm1 <- lmer(Reaction ~ Days + (Days|Subject), data=sleepstudy) arm::display(fm1) #> lmer(formula = Reaction ~ Days + (Days | Subject), data = sleepstudy) #>             coef.est coef.se #> (Intercept) 251.41     6.82  #> Days         10.47     1.55  #>  #> Error terms: #>  Groups   Name        Std.Dev. Corr  #>  Subject  (Intercept) 24.74          #>           Days         5.92    0.07  #>  Residual             25.59          #> --- #> number of obs: 180, groups: Subject, 18 #> AIC = 1755.6, DIC = 1760.3 #> deviance = 1751.9 sleepstudy[1,] #>   Reaction Days Subject #> 1   249.56    0     308 predictInterval(fm1, sleepstudy[1,], include.resid.var=0) #predict the average body fat for a group of 196cm female baseball players #>        fit      upr      lwr #> 1 253.9977 270.7438 236.2829 predictInterval(fm1, sleepstudy[1,], include.resid.var=0, ignore.fixed.terms = 1) #>        fit      upr      lwr #> 1 253.8537 268.5299 239.6275 # predict the average reaction time for a subject at day 0, taking the global intercept # (mean reaction time) as fully known predictInterval(fm1, sleepstudy[1,], include.resid.var=0, ignore.fixed.terms = "(Intercept)") #>        fit      upr      lwr #> 1 254.2354 269.3875 239.3116 #Same as above predictInterval(fm1, sleepstudy[1,], include.resid.var=0, ignore.fixed.terms = 1:2) #>        fit      upr     lwr #> 1 253.6743 269.8776 237.984 # as above, taking the first two fixed effects (intercept and days effect) as fully known predictInterval(fm1, sleepstudy[1,], include.resid.var=0,                 fix.intercept.variance = TRUE) #>        fit      upr      lwr #> 1 253.5872 268.8683 236.6639 # predict the average reaction time for a subject at day 0,, using an ad-hoc # correction for the covariance of the intercept with the random intercept effects.

code: PI.arm.time <- system.time(   PI.arm.sims <- arm::sim(fm1, 1000) )  PI.arm <- data.frame(   fit=apply(fitted(PI.arm.sims, fm1), 1, function(x) quantile(x, 0.500)),   upr=apply(fitted(PI.arm.sims, fm1), 1, function(x) quantile(x, 0.975)),   lwr=apply(fitted(PI.arm.sims, fm1), 1, function(x) quantile(x, 0.025)) )  comp.data <- rbind(data.frame(Predict.Method="predictInterval()", x=(1:nrow(PI))-0.1, PI),                    data.frame(Predict.Method="arm::sim()", x=(1:nrow(PI.arm))+0.1, PI.arm))  comp.data$Predict.Method <- factor(comp.data$Predict.Method, levels = unique(comp.data$Predict.Method)) ggplot(aes(x=x, y=fit, ymin=lwr, ymax=upr, color=Predict.Method), data=comp.data[c(1:30,181:210),]) +   geom_point() +   geom_linerange() +   labs(x="Index", y="Prediction w/ 95% PI") +   theme_bw() +  theme(legend.position="bottom") +   scale_color_brewer(type = "qual", palette = 2)

code: ##Functions for bootMer() and objects ####Return predicted values from bootstrap mySumm <- function(.) {   predict(., newdata=sleepstudy, re.form=NULL) } ####Collapse bootstrap into median, 95% PI sumBoot <- function(merBoot) {   return(     data.frame(fit = apply(merBoot$t, 2, function(x) as.numeric(quantile(x, probs=.5, na.rm=TRUE))),                lwr = apply(merBoot$t, 2, function(x) as.numeric(quantile(x, probs=.025, na.rm=TRUE))),                upr = apply(merBoot$t, 2, function(x) as.numeric(quantile(x, probs=.975, na.rm=TRUE)))     )   ) }  ##lme4::bootMer() method 1 PI.boot1.time <- system.time(   boot1 <- lme4::bootMer(fm1, mySumm, nsim=250, use.u=FALSE, type="parametric") )  PI.boot1 <- sumBoot(boot1)  comp.data <- rbind(data.frame(Predict.Method="predictInterval()", x=(1:nrow(PI))-0.1, PI),                    data.frame(Predict.Method="lme4::bootMer() - Method 1", x=(1:nrow(PI.boot1))+0.1, PI.boot1))  comp.data$Predict.Method <- factor(comp.data$Predict.Method, levels = unique(comp.data$Predict.Method)) ggplot(aes(x=x, y=fit, ymin=lwr, ymax=upr, color=Predict.Method), data=comp.data[c(1:30,181:210),]) +   geom_point() +   geom_linerange() +   labs(x="Index", y="Prediction w/ 95% PI") +   theme_bw() +  theme(legend.position="bottom") +   scale_color_brewer(type = "qual", palette = 2)

title: Validating predictInterval() against brms
text: use bundled hsb data (7,185 students 160 schools) model math achievement student SES, school-mean SES, school-varying SES slope. hold 20% students testing, plus six whole schools stand “new groups” later. draw predictive distribution held-student method – predictInterval(returnSims = TRUE) brms::posterior_predict() – compute everything (point estimates, intervals, coverage) draws.
code: data(hsb) hsb$schid <- factor(hsb$schid) new_schools <- sample(levels(hsb$schid), 6) hsb$.set <- "train" hsb$.set[hsb$schid %in% new_schools] <- "test_new" seen <- which(hsb$.set == "train") hsb$.set[sample(seen, round(0.2 * length(seen)))] <- "test_seen" train     <- droplevels(hsb[hsb$.set == "train", ]) test_seen <- hsb[hsb$.set == "test_seen" & hsb$schid %in% levels(train$schid), ] test_new  <- hsb[hsb$.set == "test_new", ]  f1 <- mathach ~ ses + meanses + (ses | schid) m_lme <- lmer(f1, data = train) brms_fit_seconds <- system.time(   m_brm <- fit_brm(f1, train, gaussian(), "brms_hsb_meanses"))[["elapsed"]] mt_seen <- mt_draws(m_lme, test_seen) pp_seen <- posterior_predict(m_brm, newdata = test_seen, allow_new_levels = TRUE)

title: Validating predictInterval() against brms

code: lme_mean <- predict(m_lme, newdata = test_seen) brm_mean <- colMeans(posterior_epred(m_brm, newdata = test_seen, allow_new_levels = TRUE)) c(correlation = cor(lme_mean, brm_mean),   mean_abs_diff = mean(abs(lme_mean - brm_mean)),   response_sd = sd(test_seen$mathach)) #>   correlation mean_abs_diff   response_sd  #>    0.99984045    0.03670134    6.71745425  ggplot(data.frame(merTools = lme_mean, brms = brm_mean), aes(merTools, brms)) +   geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = 2, color = "grey50") +   geom_point(alpha = 0.3, size = 0.8, color = "#1B9E77") + coord_equal() +   labs(x = "lme4 predict()", y = "brms posterior_epred()") +   theme_minimal(base_size = 12)

what: What about an entirely new group?
title: Validating predictInterval() against brms
text: school never seen fitting genuine modeling choice: drop school’s effect predict fixed effects , sample plausible effect estimated school-level distribution? packages support either, comparison fair choices match: Matched , two tools agree width coverage new-school intervals: interval width unseen group set choice, method. Earlier compared predictInterval()’s default brms’s default, apples--oranges pairing – apparent “gap” two packages making different default assumptions, deficiency either. much choice matters depends much group variance left fixed effects. meanses already absorbs -school variation, dropping vs. sampling school effect barely changes width. Remove meanses school SD roughly doubles, two options diverge: practical guidance: predictInterval()’s default (new.levels = "zero") answers “expected outcome typical member unseen group,” new.levels = "draw" answers “outcome unseen group drawn population” – quantity brms returns default. Pick one matches question.
code: nd <- test_new pp_pop  <- posterior_predict(m_brm, newdata = nd, re_formula = NA) pp_draw <- posterior_predict(m_brm, newdata = nd, allow_new_levels = TRUE) mt_zero <- mt_draws(m_lme, nd, new.levels = "zero") mt_draw <- mt_draws(m_lme, nd, new.levels = "draw") w <- function(d) median(qband(d, 0.90)$upr - qband(d, 0.90)$lwr) data.frame(   unseen_group   = c("drop effect", "sample effect"),   merTools_width = c(w(mt_zero), w(mt_draw)),   brms_width     = c(w(pp_pop),  w(pp_draw)),   merTools_cov90 = c(coverage(mt_zero, nd$mathach, 0.90), coverage(mt_draw, nd$mathach, 0.90)),   brms_cov90     = c(coverage(pp_pop,  nd$mathach, 0.90), coverage(pp_draw, nd$mathach, 0.90))) #>    unseen_group merTools_width brms_width merTools_cov90 brms_cov90 #> 1   drop effect       19.96801   20.02855      0.8708487  0.8745387 #> 2 sample effect       20.67910   20.76924      0.8892989  0.8856089 m0 <- lmer(mathach ~ ses + (ses | schid), data = train) data.frame(   model = c("with meanses", "without meanses"),   school_SD = c(attr(VarCorr(m_lme)$schid, "stddev")[["(Intercept)"]],                 attr(VarCorr(m0)$schid,    "stddev")[["(Intercept)"]]),   width_zero = c(w(mt_draws(m_lme, nd, new.levels = "zero")),                  w(mt_draws(m0,    nd, new.levels = "zero"))),   width_draw = c(w(mt_draws(m_lme, nd, new.levels = "draw")),                  w(mt_draws(m0,    nd, new.levels = "draw")))) #>             model school_SD width_zero width_draw #> 1    with meanses  1.598503   19.96801   20.67910 #> 2 without meanses  2.125770   19.94993   21.22576

title: Validating predictInterval() against brms
text: story carries GLMMs. fit binomial model whether grouse ticks compare predicted probabilities – predictInterval(type = "probability") brms::posterior_epred(). plot chunk glmm
code: data(grouseticks) grouseticks$TICKS_BIN <- as.integer(grouseticks$TICKS >= 1) grouseticks$cHEIGHT   <- as.numeric(scale(grouseticks$HEIGHT)) gk <- grouseticks gk$.set <- ifelse(runif(nrow(gk)) < 0.2, "test", "train") gk_tr <- droplevels(gk[gk$.set == "train", ]) gk_te <- gk[gk$.set == "test" & gk$BROOD %in% levels(gk_tr$BROOD) &             gk$LOCATION %in% unique(gk_tr$LOCATION), ]  gf <- TICKS_BIN ~ YEAR + cHEIGHT + (1 | BROOD) + (1 | LOCATION) g_lme <- glmer(gf, family = binomial, data = gk_tr,                control = glmerControl(optimizer = "bobyqa")) g_brm <- fit_brm(gf, gk_tr, bernoulli(), "brms_grouseticks")  mt_pr <- mt_draws(g_lme, gk_te, type = "probability", include.resid.var = FALSE) if (max(mt_pr) > 1 || min(mt_pr) < 0) mt_pr <- plogis(mt_pr) brm_pr <- posterior_epred(g_brm, newdata = gk_te, allow_new_levels = TRUE) mt_p <- apply(mt_pr, 2, median); brm_p <- apply(brm_pr, 2, median) c(correlation = cor(mt_p, brm_p), mean_abs_diff = mean(abs(mt_p - brm_p))) #>   correlation mean_abs_diff  #>    0.99488587    0.02704796  ggplot(data.frame(merTools = mt_p, brms = brm_p), aes(merTools, brms)) +   geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = 2, color = "grey50") +   geom_point(alpha = 0.4, color = "#7570B3") + coord_equal(xlim = c(0, 1), ylim = c(0, 1)) +   labs(title = "Predicted P(tick): merTools vs brms",        x = "predictInterval(type = 'probability')", y = "posterior_epred()") +   theme_minimal(base_size = 12)

title: Validating predictInterval() against brms
text: observed groups, predictInterval() reproduces brms’s point estimates prediction intervals almost exactly, --sample coverage, tiny fraction computational cost. new groups, choose treat unseen effect: new.levels = "zero" (default) drops predicts fixed effects, new.levels = "draw" samples estimated covariance – matching brms’s re_formula = NA allow_new_levels = TRUE respectively. Either way two packages agree; pick convention fits question. agreement holds GLMMs probability scale. short, bootstrapping full MCMC impractical, predictInterval() fast, well-calibrated stand-cases designed handle.

title: Exploring Contextual Effects with merTools
text: multilevel model, predictor measured individual level can also aggregated group level, two can carry different meanings. classic example socio-economic status (SES): student’s SES captures within-school relationship, average SES student’s school captures contextual (compositional) effect – additional influence attending less advantaged school, student’s background. vignette reproduces kind analysis described modelbased package’s article “Studying effects context” using tools merTools. data model follow contextual-effects example long used teach multilevel modeling (Raudenbush Bryk, 2002; Gelman Hill, 2007).

title: Exploring Contextual Effects with merTools
text: merTools ships hsb, subset 1982 High School Beyond survey: 7,185 students nested 160 schools. contains student-level SES measure (ses) school-mean SES (meanses) – exactly pair need separate within-school contextual effects.

title: Exploring Contextual Effects with merTools
text: model math achievement function student’s SES average SES school, allowing school intercept: coefficient ses within-school slope; coefficient meanses contextual effect. merTools::FEsim() summarizes uncertainty , plotFEsim() highlights terms whose interval excludes zero: plot chunk feplot
code: cm <- lmer(mathach ~ ses + meanses + (1 | schid), data = hsb) arm::display(cm) #> lmer(formula = mathach ~ ses + meanses + (1 | schid), data = hsb) #>             coef.est coef.se #> (Intercept) 12.66     0.15   #> ses          2.19     0.11   #> meanses      3.68     0.38   #>  #> Error terms: #>  Groups   Name        Std.Dev. #>  schid    (Intercept) 1.64     #>  Residual             6.08     #> --- #> number of obs: 7185, groups: schid, 160 #> AIC = 46578.6, DIC = 46559 #> deviance = 46563.8 fe <- FEsim(cm, n.sims = 500) plotFEsim(fe) +   labs(title = "Within-school (ses) vs. contextual (meanses) effects")

what: Predictions that separate the two effects
title: Exploring Contextual Effects with merTools
text: meanses enters model directly, can ask predicted math score changes move student across schools differing average SES holding SES fixed. wiggle() builds counterfactual data predictInterval() attaches prediction intervals: plot chunk wiggle
code: # A median student in an average school base_case <- draw(cm, type = "average") scenarios <- wiggle(base_case, varlist = "meanses",                     valueslist = list(seq(-0.7, 0.8, by = 0.15))) pi <- predictInterval(cm, newdata = scenarios, level = 0.9, n.sims = 500,                       seed = 11213) scenarios <- cbind(scenarios, pi)  ggplot(scenarios, aes(x = meanses, y = fit, ymin = lwr, ymax = upr)) +   geom_ribbon(alpha = 0.2) +   geom_line() +   labs(x = "School-average SES (context)", y = "Predicted math achievement",        title = "Contextual effect of school SES (own SES held at its mean)") +   theme_minimal(base_size = 12)

what: How much do schools matter across the rank distribution?
title: Exploring Contextual Effects with merTools
text: Finally, REimpact() summarizes much higher- versus lower-performing school shifts predicted outcome, binning schools expected rank. plotREimpact() (new release) plots result, can overlay several cases comparison: plot chunk reimpact
code: imp <- REimpact(cm, newdata = hsb[1, ], groupFctr = "schid",                 breaks = 4, n.sims = 500) plotREimpact(imp) +   labs(title = "Impact of school placement on predicted achievement")

code: data(hsb)  # Create a function to randomly assign NA values  add_NA <- function(x, prob){   z <- rbinom(length(x), 1, prob = prob)   x[z==1] <- NA   return(x) }  hsb$minority <- add_NA(hsb$minority, prob = 0.05) table(is.na(hsb$minority)) #>  #> FALSE  TRUE  #>  6832   353  hsb$female <- add_NA(hsb$female, prob = 0.05) table(is.na(hsb$female)) #>  #> FALSE  TRUE  #>  6852   333  hsb$ses <- add_NA(hsb$ses, prob = 0.05) table(is.na(hsb$ses)) #>  #> FALSE  TRUE  #>  6825   360  hsb$size <- add_NA(hsb$size, prob = 0.05) table(is.na(hsb$size)) #>  #> FALSE  TRUE  #>  6815   370 # Load imputation library library(Amelia) # Declare the variables to include in the imputation data varIndex <- names(hsb) # Declare ID variables to be excluded from imputation IDS <- c("schid", "meanses") # Let’s take case want explore impact model interaction term category continuous predictor. First, fit model interactions: Now prep data using draw function merTools. draw average observation model frame. wiggle data expanding dataframe include observation repeated different values variable specified var parameter. , expand dataset values btype, situ, Anger subsequently. next step familiar – simply pass new dataset predictInterval order generate predictions counterfactuals. plot predicted values continuous variable, Anger, facet group two categorical variables situ btype respectively.
code: data(VerbAgg) fmVA <- glmer(r2 ~ (Anger + Gender + btype + situ)^2 +            (1|id) + (1|item), family = binomial,            data = VerbAgg) #> Warning in checkConv(attr(opt, "derivs"), opt$par, ctrl = control$checkConv, : Model failed to converge with max|grad| = 0.0729926 (tol = 0.002, component 1) #>   See ?lme4::convergence and ?lme4::troubleshooting. # Select the average case newData <- draw(fmVA, type = "average") newData <- wiggle(newData, varlist = "btype",                   valueslist = list(unique(VerbAgg$btype))) newData <- wiggle(newData, var = "situ",                   valueslist = list(unique(VerbAgg$situ))) newData <- wiggle(newData, var = "Anger",                   valueslist = list(unique(VerbAgg$Anger))) head(newData, 10) #>    r2 Anger Gender btype  situ  id        item #> 1   N    20      F curse other 149 S3WantCurse #> 2   N    20      F scold other 149 S3WantCurse #> 3   N    20      F shout other 149 S3WantCurse #> 4   N    20      F curse  self 149 S3WantCurse #> 5   N    20      F scold  self 149 S3WantCurse #> 6   N    20      F shout  self 149 S3WantCurse #> 7   N    11      F curse other 149 S3WantCurse #> 8   N    11      F scold other 149 S3WantCurse #> 9   N    11      F shout other 149 S3WantCurse #> 10  N    11      F curse  self 149 S3WantCurse plotdf <- predictInterval(fmVA, newdata = newData, type = "probability",             stat = "median", n.sims = 1000) #> Warning: For binomial GLMMs, include.resid.var = TRUE simulates from the #> conditional binomial distribution (n-trial binomial simulation). #> This is the theoretically correct approach. #> To get predictions without residual variance, set include.resid.var = FALSE. plotdf <- cbind(plotdf, newData)  ggplot(plotdf, aes(y = fit, x = Anger, color = btype, group = btype)) +   geom_point() + geom_smooth(aes(color = btype), method = "lm") +   facet_wrap(~situ) + theme_bw() +   labs(y = "Predicted Probability") #> `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'

text: data.frame following five columns: groupFctr character representing name grouping factor groupLevel character representing level grouping factor term character representing formula term group estimate effect estimate lme4::ranef(, condVar=TRUE)). std.error posterior variance estimate random effect                      (lme4::ranef(, condVar=TRUE)); named "term"_var. ER expected rank. pctER percentile expected rank.

title: Calculate the expected rank of random coefficients that account for uncertainty. — expectedRank
text: Inspired Lingsma et al. (2010, see also Laird Louis 1989), expectedRank sums probability level grouping factor greater every level grouping factor, similar two-sample t-test. formula expected rank : $$ExpectedRank_i = 1 + \sum \phi((\theta_i - \theta_k) / \sqrt(var(\theta_i)+var(\theta_k))$$ \(\phi\) standard normal distribution function, \(\theta\) estimated random effect \(var(\theta)\) posterior variance estimated random effect. add one sum minimum rank one instead zero case overlap variances random effects (variances zero), expected rank equals actual rank.  ranks ordered winners ranks greater losers. formula percentile expected rank : $$100 * (ExpectedRank_i - 0.5) / N_grps$$ \(N_grps\) number grouping factor levels. percentile expected rank can interpreted fraction levels score given level. NOTE: expectedRank work conditions lme4::ranef work. One current example case models multiple terms specified per factor (e.g. uncorrelated random coefficients term, e.g. lmer(Reaction ~ Days + (1 | Subject) + (0 + Days | Subject), data = sleepstudy))

title: Calculate the expected rank of random coefficients that account for uncertainty. — expectedRank

title: Predict from merMod objects with a prediction interval — predictInterval
text: merMod merMod object lme4 newdata data.frame new data predict character specifying return, default returns full interval, can also select return fixed variation random component variation. full selected resulting data.frame nrow(newdata) * number model levels long level width prediction interval n.sims number simulation samples construct stat take median mean simulated intervals type type prediction develop include.resid.var logical, include exclude residual variance linear models returnSims logical, n.sims simulations returned? seed numeric, optional argument set seed simulations .parallel logical, use parallel computation (default FALSE) fix.intercept.variance logical; variance intercept term adjusted downwards roughly correct covariance random effects, random effects intercept effects? ignore.fixed.terms numeric string vector indexes names fixed effects considered fully known (zero variance). new.levels character, treat grouping levels newdata present model fit. "zero" (default historical behavior) drops random effect levels, prediction rests fixed effects plus residual variation. "draw" instead samples unobserved group's effect estimated random-effect covariance (VarCorr), interval reflects -group uncertainty – analogue brms::posterior_predict(allow_new_levels = TRUE). Observations share unobserved level share sampled effect.

title: merTools 1.0.0
text: 1.0 long-term-support release. resolves remaining open issues, fixes correctness bug predictInterval() nested random effects, repairs extends shinyMer() explorer, adds new plotREimpact() visualization, refreshes documentation, tidies test suite low-maintenance, long-term use.

title: merTools 1.0.0
text: predictInterval() gained new.levels argument unobserved groups. newdata contains grouping level fitted model, default new.levels = "zero" keeps historical behavior (random effect dropped, prediction rests fixed effects plus residual variation). new new.levels = "draw" instead samples unobserved group’s effect estimated random-effect covariance (VarCorr), interval reflects -group uncertainty — analogue brms::posterior_predict(allow_new_levels = TRUE). Observations sharing unobserved level share sampled effect. default unchanged, existing results identical. New plotREimpact() function visualizing REimpact() output (#84, #85). Plots weighted-average fitted value expected-rank bin grouping factor, confidence intervals, faceted case. Passing named list REimpact() results overlays shared axes influence different grouping factors (factor across models) can compared directly — previously required hand-assembling data frames. Uses clean theme_minimal() look. plotFEsim() now highlights significant fixed effects (#85). Terms whose interval excludes null line drawn solid black rest grey, matching convention already used plotREsim(), cleaner theme_minimal() canvas labelled axes. shinyMer() gained “Model Summary” tab (#78). interactive explorer now opens dedicated tab summarizing fitted model: modelInfo() overview (observations, grouping factors, AIC, residual sigma), original model call, fixed-effect estimates, random effect variances/correlations (VarCorr), number levels per grouping factor (ngrps). shinyMer() can now restrict Random / Average draw subset data (#32). “Random Obs” “Average Obs” scenario selected, sidebar control lets pick model-frame variable value filter ; chosen case drawn subset (via existing draw(..., varList = ) machinery), enabling much richer --fly exploration specific cases.

title: merTools 1.0.0
text: New “Exploring Contextual Effects merTools” vignette (#136). fresh, worked example using bundled hsb data (student SES vs. school-mean SES) separates within-group contextual effects, mirroring easystats modelbased “effects context” article (cited). demonstrates FEsim()/ plotFEsim(), wiggle() + predictInterval(), new plotREimpact() single, self-contained model. Rebuilt “Prediction Intervals” vignette fixed formatting (#116). Several section headers missing space # (rendered body text) two sub-sections numbered “Step 3c”; corrected. predictInterval()-vs-bootMer() comparison figure, longer matched surrounding text, regenerated current code now shows methods agreeing closely. latent display() call (arm, attached) qualified arm::display(). Improved package citation acknowledged influences (#137). inst/CITATION now derives version year package metadata (rather hard-coding stale version), lists package authors, citation footer points users methodological foundations — Gelman Hill (2007), arm package’s sim(), lme4 (Bates et al. 2015). package-level help page (?merTools) gained matching “Influences acknowledgements” “References” sections.

title: merTools 1.0.0
text: Fixed two shinyMer() defects “Substantive Effect” tab. fixed-effect impact (“wiggle”) plot crashed non-numeric fixed effect (object 'newvals' found), numeric/factor branch used class(x) %% ... inside (), errors “condition length > 1” multi-class objects (e.g. ordered factors) R >= 4.2. corrected (.numeric() dispatch, factor/character values now wiggle across observed levels), per-case faceting index computed correctly. app’s data table also migrated deprecated shiny::renderDataTable() DT::renderDT(), server now declares session argument, substantive-effect interval uses correct two-sided width. Fixed non-reproducible predictInterval() partially-observed nested / interaction random effects (#124). model y ~ 1 + (1 | / b), prediction frame mixing observed unobserved levels interaction grouping factor (:b) returned seed-dependent point estimates, batch predictions disagreed row--row predictions. mapping random-effect model matrix back grouping level used max.col(), returns column even -zero row (unobserved interaction level) breaks resulting tie random — unobserved level silently borrowed randomly chosen observed level’s random effect. -zero rows now detected routed existing new-level path, zeroes random-effect term’s contribution prediction falls back fixed effects plus observed higher-level random effects, exactly --sample warning describes. Observed-level predictions bit--bit unchanged. Restored single RNG stream predictInterval(). refactor predictInterval() component helpers inadvertently helper (simulate_random_effects(), simulate_fixed_effects()) call set.seed(seed) internally invoked main function, resetting RNG stream mid-call. produced numerically different output user-supplied seed compared CRAN releases. Fixed passing seed = NULL predictInterval() helpers; outer set.seed(seed) now pins single, sequential stream (sigma → random effects → fixed effects → residuals) exactly refactor. Verified origin/master 16-case harness: 14 16 cases now bit--bit identical master, remaining 2 differ intentional GLMM binomial-residual simulation fix . Fixed parse error caused debug statements inserted mid-function call predictInterval() (now correctly handles function call structure) Fixed GLMM residual variance simulation properly return NULL GLMM/NLMM models (Gaussian residual variance exists discrete distributions) Added !.null(sigma_vec) checks combine_components() handle cases GLMMs don’t Gaussian residual variance add (prevents errors sigma_vec = NULL) Removed seed parameter simulate_residual_variance() (seeds now handled predictInterval() level reproducibility) Updated test expectations reflect GLMMs return NULL simulate_residual_variance() Replaced bare subbars() reformulas::subbars() random-effect prediction path resolve deprecation warning lme4, migrated subbars reformulas package. Fixed predictInterval() models multiple random-effect term blocks per grouping factor (#118). Models using double-bar syntax ((x + y || g)), explicit splits ((1|g) + (0 + x|g)), mixed correlated + uncorrelated specs previously failed Error dimnames(reMatrix) <- *vtmp* : 'dimnames' applied non-array lme4::ranef(..., condVar = TRUE) returns postVar list per-block arrays cases, level-filtering code assumed 3-D array. simulate_random_effects() now normalizes list single block-diagonal array (zero -diagonals uncorrelated blocks, preserving full covariance within correlated blocks) indexing, mvtnorm::rmvnorm() path sees mathematically correct joint posterior covariance. Correlated-models unaffected (guarded .list() check). Fixed averageObs() / findFormFuns() matrix-LHS models (#83). averageObs(gm1) previously errored two-column binomial GLMMs glmer(cbind(successes, failures) ~ ..., family = binomial) collapseFrame() attempted take mean matrix response column, latent bug weights-selection path tried index (weights) column exist model frame cbind specifications. Matrix response columns now detected dropped averaging. Behavior change: matrix-LHS models returned frame longer contains response column; scalar-LHS models response still included . Callers key column count column names averageObs() treat matrix-LHS output predictors-. output remains valid newdata predict() predictInterval(), ignore response column.

title: merTools 1.0.0
text: LMMs: Gaussian residual variance via rnorm(N, yhat, sigma) gamma-distributed sigma GLMMs: Conditional distribution simulation via simulate_glmm_response() (binomial/poisson/gamma) include.resid.var = TRUE GLMM type = "probability": Simulates conditional distribution (theoretically correct discrete distributions) include.resid.var = TRUE GLMM type = "linear.prediction": Returns linear predictor without Gaussian noise (correct behavior - GLMMs don’t additive Gaussian noise)

title: merTools 1.0.0
text: Added tests/comparisons/predictInterval-regression.R, standalone cross-version numeric regression harness. pins canonical set LMM GLMM inputs — covering , level, stat, ignore.fixed.terms, fix.intercept.variance, single-row-newdata cases — serializes predictInterval() output RDS bundle two package versions can compared bit--bit via diff subcommand. Invoke whenever touching simulation internals confirm change silently alter user-facing numeric output. See README worked git worktree-based workflow. Pinned RNG version algorithm across R releases via new tests/testthat/helper-seed.R sets RNGversion("4.1.0") explicit RNGkind(). prevents silent stream differences across R-oldrel / R-release / R-devel CI. Disabled testthat parallel execution (Config/testthat/parallel: false). File-level set.seed() behaves unpredictably parallel workers separate RNG state, root cause several intermittent CI failures observed 0.9.0 development cycle. Unified test seeds 11213 consistency, preserving single differing seed two tests explicitly assert different seeds produce different results. Refactored thetaExtract() test test-helpers.R brittle numeric-equality check value calibrated older seed, behavioral assertions (type, length, bounds).
